summary: 11 concrete tools, models, and side projects surfaced from product-rich HN comment threads.

items.name: REAPER
items.summary: Lightweight cross-platform DAW that commenters praised for tiny installs and serious capability.
items.why_included: HN turned a nostalgia thread into a strong recommendation for a still-relevant audio workstation.
items.evidence: Commenters highlighted REAPER’s small installer, generous trial model, and lineage from Justin Frankel’s Nullsoft era.

items.name: NSIS
items.summary: Scripted Windows installer system still favored for silent, practical deployments.
items.why_included: Commenters treated it as a durable installer tool worth using instead of heavier commercial options.
items.evidence: The thread praised its assembly-like scripting, Dialog Designer, and hands-on installer workflow.

items.name: NINJAM
items.summary: Internet jamming tool that turns latency into part of the musical workflow.
items.why_included: HN treated it as a clever collaboration idea rather than a throwaway Nullsoft footnote.
items.evidence: Comments described musicians jamming over the internet by leaning into delayed measures instead of fighting network lag.

items.name: TypedMemory
items.source_story_title: Library for fast mapping of Java records to native memory
items.summary: Java library that maps records onto native memory for low-overhead access.
items.why_included: The thread focused on its performance tradeoffs and the unusual bytecode-generation design.
items.evidence: HN commenters compared it with SBE, Span<T>, Graal, and Java memory APIs while discussing hidden classes and zero-reflection access.

items.name: Ratty
items.source_story_title: Ratty – A terminal emulator with inline 3D graphics
items.summary: Rust terminal emulator that renders inline 3D graphics.
items.why_included: It drew both enthusiasm and serious debate about richer terminal UX.
items.evidence: Comments tied it to TempleOS, kitty graphics, Ghostty, and the broader question of whether terminals should carry 3D context.

items.name: CUDA-oxide
items.source_story_title: CUDA-oxide: Nvidia's official Rust to CUDA compiler
items.summary: NVIDIA’s Rust-to-CUDA compiler stack for GPU kernels.
items.why_included: HN treated it as a serious Rust GPU path with a nontrivial safety model.
items.evidence: The discussion covered the MIR-to-PTX pipeline, kernel-argument checks, and slice/safety abstractions like DisjointSlice.

items.name: Interfaze
items.source_story_title: Interfaze: A new model architecture built for high accuracy at scale
items.summary: Task-specific model architecture for OCR, translation, and GUI detection.
items.why_included: A commenter reported unusually strong OCR on a hard scanned page, which made the thread feel concrete.
items.evidence: The HN discussion debated bounding boxes, structured output, and where specialized DNNs beat general-purpose LLMs.

items.name: Guitar tuner that uses phone accelerometer
items.source_story_title: Guitar tuner that uses phone accelerometer
items.summary: Web guitar tuner that reads phone vibrations with the accelerometer.
items.why_included: HN liked the sensor trick even while questioning its tuning reliability.
items.evidence: Comments discussed autocorrelation, aliasing, and the privacy implications of high-frequency sensor access.

items.name: Ghostty
items.summary: Modern terminal emulator suggested as a practical alternative.
items.why_included: It was the clearest terminal alternative name-check in the discussion.
items.evidence: A commenter asked “Why not try Ghostty?” and the follow-up debate centered on packaging availability and terminal tradeoffs.

items.name: timg
items.summary: Terminal image and video viewer for quick shell-side previews.
items.why_included: A commenter framed it as a practical way to show images directly in the terminal.
items.evidence: The thread called out easy installation and immediate terminal previews with `brew install timg`.

items.name: yazi
items.summary: TUI file browser with built-in image and file previews.
items.why_included: HN pointed to it as a concrete workflow upgrade for terminal-heavy users.
items.evidence: Comments recommended it for image previews and broader file browsing inside the shell.
